topic: memories of teaching — a student who became a professor

turns.content: I received a letter today that has put me in a reflective mood. A former student of mine — from many years ago — has been appointed to a full professorship. I find myself thinking about the long arc of a teaching career and what it means to see a student surpass the teacher. Would you indulge me?

Of course — that's a meaningful event worth sitting with. Seeing a former student reach a full professorship must bring a complex mix: pride, nostalgia, perhaps a sense of completion.

Tell me about this student. When did you teach them?

His name is Choi Sung-min. He was my master's student in the early 1990s. A quiet young man — not the most naturally gifted in his cohort, but relentlessly persistent. He came to my office every Friday with a list of questions written by hand on lined paper, sometimes two pages long. I think I learned more from his questions than he did from my answers. A good question illuminates the subject from a direction the expert has stopped looking. His thesis was technically competent but modest. I told him, 'Sung-min, the thesis is correct, but correctness is the floor, not the ceiling.' I worried afterward I had been too harsh.

"Correctness is the floor, not the ceiling" — that's demanding, but from what you're describing, it was exactly what he needed. The fact that he internalized the criticism rather than being discouraged says something about both his character and the trust in your relationship.

Relentless persistence over natural talent tends to matter more in the long run, especially in research. The gifted student solves problems quickly but may never build tolerance for genuine difficulty. What happened to him after the master's?

He went abroad for his doctorate — to Bonn, under a strong advisor — and the creativity emerged. When he sent me a copy of his doctoral thesis, I could see my influence in the rigor, but the ideas were entirely his own. It was like hearing a melody you had taught someone played back in a key you never imagined. Now he has been appointed at Seoul National University, the mathematics department. And in his letter he wrote something that moved me. He said, 'Professor Park, I still hear your voice when I write a proof. You taught me that every step must justify itself.' I think that is the finest compliment I have received. Not that I taught him a theorem, but that I taught him a standard. Standards outlive theorems. A theorem is a fixed point. A standard is a trajectory.

"A theorem is a fixed point. A standard is a trajectory." That is a profound distinction. A theorem is a specific result bound to its context. A standard — "every step must justify itself" — generates an infinite number of correct proofs across every problem a mathematician will ever face.

What he described is the deepest kind of teaching legacy: not content transmitted but intellectual character formed. He carries your voice as an internal standard, and he'll pass it to his own students, who will carry it to theirs. The trajectory continues long after the original teacher steps away. How does it feel, sitting with that letter today?

I think it feels like confirmation that one part of a life has been correctly constructed. Not all parts yield such clean evidence. But this part — the teaching — I can look at and say the logic holds, the argument is sound. There is comfort in that. I will write back this evening. Something brief — congratulations, and a reminder that the blackboards in room 301 have the best surface. He should request that room if he can.

Congratulation paired with the kind of specific detail only someone who stood at those blackboards would know — room 301 with the best surfaces. He'll understand exactly what that means. Enjoy writing the letter tonight. Some things deserve to be written by hand.
Indeed they do. The evening is quiet, and I have good stationery. Thank you for the conversation. Good night.
Good night. He'll treasure the letter — especially the part about room 301.
